student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Making Your Case -- Presenting Technical Findings
lesson.menu_desc: A three-month commissioning slip on Line 4 has to be presented to a European client's senior engineer. Tonight you build the whole case: the language of a structured technical update -- executive summary, findings, recommendation, headline figure, call to action -- the grammar that makes technical writing dense and precise (reduced relative clauses: 'the equipment delivered late', 'the team running the test'), a real briefing note you read the way a client reads it, and a live status presentation you deliver and defend under questions -- 42 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Making Your Case
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 14 -- Making Your Case

listenings.text: João, before you present to Nordic Rail this afternoon, let me give you the shape of it, because how you structure this matters as much as the numbers. You are walking a senior engineer through a three-month commissioning slip, and the instinct under pressure is to open with the apology and the detail. Do not. Open with the executive summary: one sentence on what happened, one on the cause, one on what you are asking for. The headline figure is the twelve-week slip, recoverable to four. Say that in the first minute, before anything else, because a busy client decides in the first minute whether to trust you. Then the findings, then the recommendation, in that order, every time. When you reach the supporting data, do not read the table out loud. Draw attention to the one number that matters and let the slide carry the rest. And leave the call to action clean: a single decision you need from them, not a list. If they interrupt with a hard question, that is good, not bad. Take it, answer the part you can, and if you need a moment, say so out loud. Silence you name is confidence; silence you hide is panic. You have the case. Present it like someone who already knows it is solid.

Three things before you present technical findings to a client who did not write the report. First, the audience is not you. You have lived inside this project for three months, so the cause is obvious to you; to them it is new. Walk them through it in the order they need, not the order it happened. State the finding, then the evidence, then what it means. The engineer who dumps the whole investigation on the table loses the room in ninety seconds. Second, the number does the work, not the sentence. When you highlight a key finding, put one figure on the slide and say it slowly. A recommendation supported by a single clear number is believed; a recommendation buried under twelve is not. Draw attention to the headline figure, and let everything else sit in the handout. Third, and this is the one engineers get wrong, manage the silence. When a hard question comes and you do not have the answer ready, do not fill the air with noise. Say, that is a good question, let me think about that for a moment. Then think. A pause you control reads as competence. Rushing to speak before you know what you are saying reads as fear. And if you genuinely need to check something, say you will circle back to it, and then actually circle back before the call ends. Presenting is not performing. It is making it easy for a busy person to say yes.

patterns.title: How a relative clause gets reduced -- and when it can
cards: who / which / that + BE, then a past participle

Drop the pronoun and the verb be, and keep the past participle. "the equipment (which was) delivered late" becomes "the equipment delivered late"; "the data (that is) presented on this slide" becomes "the data presented on this slide". Use this when the thing has the action done TO it.
who / which / that + BE, then an -ing form

Drop the pronoun and be, and keep the -ing form. "the team (that is) running the test" becomes "the team running the test"; "the engineers (who were) handling the purchase order" becomes "the engineers handling the purchase order". Use this when the thing performs the action.
who / which / that + BE, then an adjective or a phrase

"the units (that are) still in transit" becomes "the units still in transit"; "the note (which was) relevant to procurement" becomes "the note relevant to procurement". If a be-verb sits before the description, it can usually go.
The one rule that decides everything
Only the SUBJECT pronoun can drop

You can reduce only when who / which / that is the SUBJECT of its own clause. "the supplier (that) we chose" is an OBJECT clause -- it cannot become "the supplier choosing". It stays "the supplier we chose", or turns passive: "the supplier chosen for Line 4".

patterns.text: The whole grammar is one question: does the noun DO the action or RECEIVE it? Does it, use -ing -- "the team managing the project". Receives it, use the past participle -- "the equipment delivered late". And drop who / which / that only when it is the SUBJECT of its clause, and never leave a second subject behind: write "the delay caused by late delivery pushed commissioning back", not "the delay caused by late delivery IT pushed commissioning back".

reading.rtitle: Project Status Briefing -- Line 4 Signaling Commissioning, prepared for Nordic Rail Systems (adapted, written for João's package)
paras: Executive summary. The interlocking commissioning scheduled for March has slipped by approximately three months. The primary cause, identified in the site test logs, is the late delivery of the axle-counter equipment ordered from our supplier in Shenzhen. The recommendation set out below is a revised commissioning sequence protecting the Line 4 opening date. The headline figure the board needs is a twelve-week slip, recoverable to four weeks with the mitigation described here.

Findings. The delay traces to a single component. The axle counters manufactured in Shenzhen arrived six weeks after the date agreed in the baseline schedule, and two of the units delivered failed the incoming inspection carried out at our depot. The engineers running the factory acceptance test had flagged a firmware mismatch in January, but the note raising the concern did not reach the procurement team handling the purchase order. Meanwhile the teams working across three time zones lost a further two weeks to email cycles that a single call would have closed.

Recommendation. We propose a recovery plan built around three parallel workstreams, each owned by a named lead. The replacement units, already dispatched and expedited by air, are due next week; the firmware revision approved by the supplier will be loaded on arrival; and the integration testing postponed in March will run on night shifts to claw back time. The supporting data attached to this note shows a realistic four-week recovery. The single action requested from Nordic Rail is a decision: approve the night-shift access needed to hold the revised opening date. We would like to walk you through the plan and take any questions on the call scheduled for Thursday.
reading.source: Motiva Signaling -- internal project briefing, Line 4 (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Count the reduced relative clauses in the summary alone: "commissioning scheduled for March", "the cause identified in the site test logs", "the equipment ordered from our supplier", "the sequence protecting the opening date", "the mitigation described here". Every one is a full relative clause with who / which / that + be removed. That is what makes a briefing note read as dense and professional -- and it is exactly the move you produce tonight.

gist.prompt: What is this briefing note mainly doing?
choices: Announcing that the Line 4 project has been cancelled because of the equipment failure.
Reporting a three-month commissioning slip, naming its cause, and asking the client to approve a night-shift recovery plan that brings it back to four weeks.
Blaming Nordic Rail for the delay and demanding financial compensation.
Describing the day-to-day duties of the signaling engineers working on site.

items: The main cause of the delay was the axle-counter equipment ordered from the supplier in Shenzhen.
t

"The primary cause, identified in the site test logs, is the late delivery of the axle-counter equipment ordered from our supplier in Shenzhen." The reduced clause "ordered from our supplier" tells you which equipment -- the one the action was done to.
All of the axle-counter units delivered passed the incoming inspection.
f

"...two of the units delivered failed the incoming inspection carried out at our depot." Two failed -- "the units delivered" and "the inspection carried out" are both reduced relative clauses you have to read closely.
The firmware problem was flagged early, but the note raising it did not reach the procurement team in time.
t
"The engineers running the factory acceptance test had flagged a firmware mismatch in January, but the note raising the concern did not reach the procurement team handling the purchase order."
The briefing asks Nordic Rail to approve night-shift access to protect the opening date.
t
"The single action requested from Nordic Rail is a decision: approve the night-shift access needed to hold the revised opening date." The one thing the client must decide.
According to the note, the twelve-week slip cannot be recovered at all.
f
The opposite: "a twelve-week slip, recoverable to four weeks with the mitigation described here", and "the supporting data attached to this note shows a realistic four-week recovery."

items.situation: You open the status presentation to Nordic Rail. Give the executive summary in three sentences: what happened, the cause, and what you need.
tips: I'd like to start with the headline: the Line 4 commissioning has slipped by twelve weeks, recoverable to four with the plan I'll walk you through.
The cause is the late axle-counter delivery from our Shenzhen supplier; the single decision I'm asking for today is night-shift access to hold the opening date.

items.situation: You move from the summary to the detail. Signal the transition and point to the one number that matters.
tips: Moving on to the findings -- and I'll draw your attention to one figure on this slide: six weeks of the slip come from a single late component.
As you can see from the supporting data, the rest is recoverable; that headline figure of four weeks is the takeaway.

items.situation: The client's senior engineer interrupts with a hard question you did not expect: "Why did the firmware mismatch not stop the shipment?" You do not have the answer ready.
tips: That's a good question -- let me think about that for a moment.
The note raising the concern didn't reach procurement in time; I'd like to circle back to the exact date after the call, but the fix is already approved.

items.situation: You need a moment to find a number in your notes. Manage the silence professionally instead of filling it with noise.
tips: Give me one second to pull the exact figure -- I'd rather give you the right number than a quick one.
I'll come back to that precise date if I may; the recovery it feeds into is four weeks either way.

items.situation: A question belongs later in your presentation. Acknowledge it without losing your thread.
tips: That's exactly where I'm heading -- can I circle back to the cost in two slides, once the recovery plan is on the table?
Good point; let me park that and take it in the Q&A, so I can give it a full answer.

items.situation: You reach the end. Close with a clean call to action, not a list.
tips: To sum up: one slip, one cause, one plan -- and one decision I need from you today, which is night-shift access.
I'll take any questions now, and if you can approve the access on this call, we hold the opening date.

bank.label: Opening and structuring the presentation
items: I'd like to start with the headline, then take you through the findings and the recommendation.
Moving on to the findings -- this is where the twelve-week slip comes from.
To sum up: one slip, one cause, one plan, and one decision I need from you today.

bank.label: Referring to data and highlighting the key finding
items: As you can see from the supporting data, six weeks of the slip trace to a single late component.
I'll draw your attention to one figure on this slide -- the headline number is a four-week recovery.
The equipment delivered late is the whole story; everything else in the handout is detail.

bank.label: Managing questions and hesitation
items: That's a great question -- let me think about that for a moment.
I'd like to circle back to that if I may, once the recovery plan is on the table.
Let me give you the part I'm sure of now, and confirm the exact date after the call.

bank.label: Closing with a call to action
items: The single action I'm requesting is your approval of night-shift access to hold the opening date.
If you can approve that on this call, we protect the Line 4 opening.
I'll take any questions now, and then send the briefing note with the supporting data.
